old_claim.note: Basic Rope Making is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim.note: Sails require rigging lines, stays, sheets, and halyards to support masts and control sail angle; cordage enables that sail-control system.

source_shape.source_locator: Article defines rigging as a sailing vessel's lines or cordage and explains running rigging ropes used to raise, lower, trim, and shorten sails.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source identifies cordage and lines as functional rigging components used to support and control sails.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports changing rope from a loose historical predecessor to an enabling capability for the mast, stay, halyard, and sheet system that makes sail control work.

ontology_before: The graph treated rope making as merely an earlier background technology for sailing.
ontology_after: The graph treats rope making as an enabling component capability because rigging lines and cordage support and control sails.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: the sailing to basic_rope_making edge is now enabling with textbook evidence.
would_reject_if: The sailing node is narrowed to a sail depiction without any implied rigging or sail-control system.
A source shows early sailing could be modeled without any line, stay, sheet, or halyard capability.
The basic_rope_making node is narrowed away from usable cordage.
short_reason: Cordage enables sail rigging rather than serving only as distant background.
